current_fact.fact_text: On 24 March, the world also marks the International Day for the Right to the Truth Concerning Gross Human Rights Violations, designated by the UN in honor of Óscar Romero. It’s a reminder that uncovering what really happened—names, dates, and stories—is itself a form of justice.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.

current_fact.match_reason: A day dedicated to truth and memory fits an introspective episode about seeing clearly through our digital habits, questioning what connects us and what distorts reality.

queue.fact_text: On 24 March 1958, Elvis Presley was drafted into the U.S. Army, abruptly swapping the spotlight for a standard-issue uniform. Many feared his career would fizzle during his service, but the two-year break only fed the mythos around rock’s first global superstar.

episode.title: Morning Drops #36 - Women of Rock
episode.description: A tribute to the women who shaped the sound of rock, the ones who picked up a guitar and refused to ask for permission. The conversation unfolds in Italian, led by Gaia and her father. A dialogue between generations and a reminder that the pulse of rock has always belonged to anyone brave enough to play it loud. Distortion, rhythm, freedom.
songs: Joan Jett & The Blackhearts - I Love Rock 'n Roll

queue.match_reason: Elvis’s conscription is a turning point in rock history; pairing it with an episode celebrating rock’s evolution and its boundary-breaking players broadens the story beyond one king to many queens.

queue.fact_text: On 24 March 1989, the Exxon Valdez oil tanker struck Bligh Reef in Alaska’s Prince William Sound, releasing millions of gallons of crude oil into a pristine marine ecosystem. The spill blackened coastlines, devastated wildlife, and helped galvanize modern environmental activism and stricter shipping regulations.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

queue.match_reason: A catastrophic oil spill in cold northern waters pairs powerfully with an episode immersed in Arctic seas and marine life, highlighting both the beauty and vulnerability of ocean ecosystems.

queue.fact_text: On 24 March 2004, physicists at Brookhaven National Laboratory announced evidence for a new form of ordinary matter: a “perfect” liquid created in heavy-ion collisions that behaves more like a frictionless fluid than a gas of particles. This quark–gluon plasma briefly recreates conditions microseconds after the Big Bang, inside a metal ring on Long Island.

episode.title: Morning Drops #48 - Cosmic Monday
episode.description:
Cosmic Monday is a Morning Drops episode built around four Italian dance records, tracing how disco rhythms slowly became electronic language.
Gaia moves through four tracks like chapters: groove first, then machines, then that bright, futuristic feeling you only get when the bassline is doing the talking. It's not nostalgia, exactly. It's more like returning to a sound that still works — especially on mornings when you need a little extra energy, but you don't want anything aggressive.
Take it as a slow transmission. Let it carry you into the day.
songs: Giovanotti Mondani Meccanici - Don't Ask Me Why

queue.match_reason: A laboratory recreation of early-universe matter, described as a nearly perfect liquid, matches the futuristic, cosmic-leaning dance textures of an episode titled “Cosmic Monday,” both touching the edges of the universe through human-made machines.

published.fact_text: On 24 March 1993, astronomers Carolyn and Eugene Shoemaker, along with David Levy, discovered Comet Shoemaker–Levy 9 at Palomar Observatory in California. The comet later fragmented and famously crashed into Jupiter in 1994, giving scientists a rare chance to watch an extraterrestrial collision reshape a giant planet’s atmosphere in real time.

episode.title: Morning Drops #03 - Space Shift
episode.description: Gaia invites you to shift your mindset with a simple yet powerful reflection: What if we saw emptiness not as a void but as space to create? An energising and feel-good selection of tracks, the perfect way to ease into your day and set the tone for the week ahead.
songs: Willy J Healey - Woke Up Smiling
The Strokes - You Only Live Once

published.match_reason: A cosmic discovery about a comet and planetary impacts pairs naturally with an episode themed around "space" and creative emptiness, echoing the vastness of the solar system.

published.fact_text: On 24 March 1721, Johann Sebastian Bach dedicated a set of six concertos to Margrave Christian Ludwig of Brandenburg-Schwedt, works that later became known as the Brandenburg Concertos. These pieces, forgotten for more than a century, are now celebrated as some of the most inventive and joyful orchestral music of the Baroque era.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Endless Summer
episode.description: Recorded from Fano, a quiet town on the Adriatic coast where summer stretches long and slow. Between swims, simple meals, and time with family, Gaia reflects on the kind of music that helps us return to ourselves. Through different shades of jazz — from minimal and meditative to warm and rhythmic — an invitation to slip into that endless summer feeling.

published.match_reason: Bach’s intricate, timeless concertos resonate with an episode built around sophisticated, enduring instrumental music and a sense of calm, crafted structure.

published.fact_text: On 24 March 1992, Space Shuttle Atlantis launched on mission STS-45, carrying the first Atmospheric Laboratory for Applications and Science (ATLAS-1). For nine days, astronauts orbited Earth studying the planet’s atmosphere and the Sun, turning the shuttle into a flying observatory for our fragile air envelope.

episode.title: Morning Drops #40 - Diving With Orcas
episode.description: Yasmin's friend Caroline takes us beyond the polar circle, into the Arctic waters of Norway, where orcas and humpback whales gather each winter. A personal journey shaped by a quiet obsession with orcas, from Patagonia to the icy fjords of the north. The soundscape moves gently between cinematic swells and soft, rhythmic pulses — echoing the cold light of Arctic mornings and the grace of underwater encounters.

published.match_reason: A shuttle mission studying Earth’s atmosphere mirrors the episode’s theme of exploring Earth’s extreme environments from another perspective, both turning the planet into an object of awe and observation.

published.fact_text: On 24 March 1882, Robert Koch announced his discovery of Mycobacterium tuberculosis, the bacterium responsible for tuberculosis. At a time when TB was one of the deadliest diseases on Earth, identifying its microscopic cause transformed it from a mysterious curse into a solvable scientific puzzle.

episode.title: Morning Drops #42 - The Joy Of Learning
episode.description:
Hosted by Yasmin, this Morning Drop explores learning as a lifelong process, focusing on persistence, repetition, and the emotional landscape that comes with challenging oneself over time.

Through personal stories and scientific insights, the episode looks at how learning reshapes the brain, generates joy through effort, and why progress often arrives after confusion and frustration. It reflects on the so-called delayed pleasure effect, the role of dopamine in motivation, and how curiosity and learning support long-term wellbeing.

The episode also questions our cultural relationship with failure and invites a softer, more realistic approach to learning, one that accepts mistakes as part of growth and sees persistence as a quiet form of strength.

Mood: reflective, grounded, quietly hopeful
Music genre: alternative rock, hip hop, electronic, neo-soul, drill

A space to listen slowly and stay with the process.

published.match_reason: Koch’s painstaking research embodies the power of methodical learning and scientific curiosity, perfectly echoing an episode that dives into the emotional science of learning and discovery.

published.fact_text: On the night of 24 March 1944, 76 Allied prisoners of war crawled through a hand-dug tunnel out of the German camp Stalag Luft III, an audacious breakout later dramatized as “The Great Escape.” The operation involved months of secret engineering underground, from air-pumping systems to hidden workshops for forging documents.

episode.title: Morning Drops #49 - Rage Against The Machine
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin revisits the story of Rage Against the Machine — a band born in tension, shaped by conviction, and remembered for moments that blurred the line between stage and protest.
From early Los Angeles unrest to Wall Street performances, this episode moves through rupture, resistance, and legacy — carried by songs that still feel urgent today.
Listen closely.
songs: Rage Against the Machine - Killing in the Name
Rage Against the Machine - Without a Face
Rage Against the Machine - Guerrilla Radio
Rage Against the Machine - Sleep Now in the Fire

published.match_reason: A daring escape from a prison camp is an act of resistance in wartime, well matched with an episode centered on a band whose entire identity is built around defiance and political rebellion.

published.fact_text: On 24 March 1976, Argentina’s armed forces overthrew President Isabel Perón and began the National Reorganization Process, a seven-year military dictatorship. Behind the bureaucratic name lay a brutal campaign of disappearances, censorship, and fear that reshaped the country’s political and cultural landscape.

episode.title: Morning Drops #44 - Residente
episode.description:
In this edition of Morning Drops, Yasmin navigates the complex geography of René Pérez Joglar, known to the world as Residente. His story is not just a musical evolution; it is a timeline marked by resistance, lyrical obsession, and a relentless search for identity.

From the gated streets of Trujillo Alto to the global stage, we explore how a restless mind transformed into the voice of a generation. The selection moves through the textures of his life: hearing the raw energy of his early rebellion, the sharp precision of his political critique, and finally, the fragile silence of his most personal confessions.

It is a study in duality. We witness the rapper who dismantles giants and the son who misses his mother. It is a soundscape that proves that sometimes, the loudest revolution happens inside.

Mood: Raw, Revolutionary, Introspective, Honest
Music genre: Latin Alternative, Hip Hop, Urban, World

published.match_reason: A Latin American dictatorship and its legacy of repression connect strongly to Residente’s politically charged, Latin-rooted storytelling about power, memory, and resistance.

published.fact_text: On 24 March 1927, during the Nanking Incident, foreign warships bombarded the Chinese city of Nanjing to protect foreign residents amid chaotic anti-foreigner riots. The clash unfolded as China’s political landscape was fracturing and foreign powers were still exerting heavy influence along its rivers and ports.

episode.title: Morning Drops #39 - Moving Meditation
episode.description: An episode inspired by how travel helps us see more clearly. Stepping into new places opens the mind, shifts perspective. The music moves through balearic warmth, boogie grooves, world and latin-folk influences, and touches of new wave — a selection built to follow the quiet changes that happen when you're out of your usual rhythm.
songs: España Circo Este - Dormo poco e sogno molto
Talking Heads - This Must Be the Place (Naive Melody)

published.match_reason: An event shaped by foreign presence and global tension contrasts with an episode about travel and perspective, inviting listeners to reflect on how movement across borders can be either violent or transformative.

published.fact_text: On 24 March 1980, Salvadoran Archbishop Óscar Romero was assassinated while celebrating Mass in San Salvador after speaking out against state violence and injustice. His final homily became a last act of defiance, turning him into a symbol of faith-driven human rights activism across Latin America and beyond.

episode.title: Morning Drops #34 - Soul Sisters
episode.description: A tribute to four iconic voices — women who have shaped the way we listen, feel, and move. Each track carries a different kind of presence: one that holds you, one that questions, one that grounds, and one that lets go. An energy that is soulful, raw, and unapologetically feminine.
songs: Grace Jones - Walking in the Rain
Aretha Franklin - A Rose Is Still a Rose

published.match_reason: Romero’s moral voice and sacrifice align with an episode honoring powerful, principled voices; both explore how human courage and conviction can move entire communities.

published.fact_text: On 24 March 1977, Morarji Desai became prime minister of India, the first person to hold the office who did not belong to the Indian National Congress. His election marked a rare interruption in the party’s post-independence dominance and a moment of political rebalancing after the Emergency period.

episode.title: Morning Drops #20 - Hitchhiking Poland
episode.description: Yasmin takes you back to the summer of 2020, when borders were still uncertain and a long-held wish to visit Poland finally came true. Through four tracks, tracing her journey from a stranger's couch in Poznań to the peaks of Zakopane.

published.match_reason: A political shift in one vast democracy mirrors a personal journey across another country navigating borders and change, with both stories involving transitions, new directions, and regional identities.

published.fact_text: On 24 March 1946, a British Cabinet Mission arrived in India to negotiate the transfer of power from the British Raj to Indian leaders. Their proposals, which tried to balance unity with autonomy, ultimately failed to prevent Partition but marked a key step toward the end of colonial rule.

episode.title: Morning Drops #45 - It Can't Rain Forever
episode.description:
In this first broadcast of 2026, Gaia returns from the vivid landscapes of Morocco to open a space for reflection. This episode navigates the delicate transition between closing a significant chapter and stepping into the new year. It explores the concept of constancy amidst change, celebrating the grounding force of friendship and the creative sanctuary that is Sedna FM.

The musical selection mirrors this journey, moving from the orchestral depth of changing seasons into the golden warmth of psychedelic soul. It recalls memories of Swiss mountains and unexpected downpours, reminding us that shared optimism can weather any storm.

Take a moment to breathe in the new morning and listen deeply.

Mood: Reflective, Warm, Transitional, Hopeful
Music genre: Classic Rock, Psychedelic Soul, Pop Rock, Soft Rock

published.match_reason: A historic negotiation about political transition and an episode about weathering storms and moving into a new chapter both revolve around change, uncertainty, and cautious hope.

published.fact_text: On 24 March 1794, in Kraków, Tadeusz Kościuszko proclaimed a nationwide uprising against Imperial Russia and the Kingdom of Prussia and assumed command of Polish forces. His call for resistance blended military strategy with a promise of social reforms, making him a symbol of both national and popular struggle.

episode.title: Morning Drops #16 - The Sound of Tuareg
episode.description: A journey into the sound of the Tuareg: from traditional chants and desert rituals to the electrified rhythms of exile and resistance. Music shaped by sand, memory, and survival.

published.match_reason: Kościuszko’s uprising and the Tuareg’s musical traditions of resistance both tell stories of people fighting for autonomy and dignity against powerful empires.

published.fact_text: On 24 March 2018, students across the United States organized the March for Our Lives, one of the largest youth-led protests in American history, to demand gun control after the Parkland high school shooting. Teens who had survived violence in their classrooms became national voices pushing for policy change.

episode.title: Morning Drops #04 - Bars With Balls
episode.description: Yasmin dives into Women's Day through the voices of four incredible female rappers. Morning fire from the magic planet of Sedna FM — move your hands up and down with us. Hip hop culture through a feminine lens.
songs: Little Simz - Point and Kill (feat. Obongjayar)

published.match_reason: A youth-led movement demanding safety and rights fits the energy of an episode celebrating outspoken rappers who turn personal experience into political voice and empowerment.

published.fact_text: On 24 March 1999, NATO launched air strikes against Yugoslavia without UN Security Council approval during the Kosovo War, the first time in its history the alliance attacked a sovereign state. The operation sparked intense debate over humanitarian intervention, sovereignty, and the rules of war in the post–Cold War era.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - A Trip-Hop Journey
episode.description: Hosted by friend Lorenzo, a journey through the roots and evolution of trip-hop. From Bristol's early experiments with Massive Attack and Tricky, to DJ Shadow's cinematic sampling in California, and Thievery Corporation's global downtempo grooves in Washington D.C. — exploring how this genre blurred boundaries and reshaped electronic music.
songs: Tricky - Hell Is Round the Corner
DJ Shadow - Midnight in a Perfect World

published.match_reason: A controversial military campaign that blurred moral and legal boundaries parallels an episode about a genre that blurs musical borders and creates uneasy, cinematic atmospheres.

published.fact_text: On 24 March 1998, German surgeon Rüdiger Marmulla performed the first computer-assisted bone segment navigation procedure at the University of Regensburg. By combining imaging and real-time tracking, surgeons could move bone fragments with millimetre precision—turning the operating theatre into something closer to a 3D engineering lab.

episode.title: Morning Drops #47 - The Rise Of Autotune
episode.description:
In this Morning Drop, Yasmin explores the explosive rise of Auto-Tune, a tool that quietly changed the sound of music without asking for attention. From fragile, imperfect recordings to voices shaped with precision, this episode follows the moment when correction became standard and sound became controlled.

Along the way, we discover the curious story of how it was invented, how it moved from the background of studios to the center of debate, and how a few artists turned it into something expressive rather than hidden.

Listen slowly, and let us know what you think about Autotune.
songs: Chet Baker – I Fall in Love Too Easily

published.match_reason: A pioneering surgery using digital navigation mirrors Auto-Tune’s transformation of vocal performance—both stories about technology quietly reprogramming what precision means in human craft.

published.fact_text: On 24 March 1982, Bangladeshi President Abdus Sattar was removed in a bloodless coup led by Army Chief Hussain Muhammad Ershad, who suspended the constitution and imposed martial law. The sudden shift highlighted how fragile young democracies can be when military power and civilian rule collide.

episode.title: Morning Drops #35 - Thailand
episode.description: Recorded in Koh Samui, Thailand, where Yasmin and her friend Patricia spent more than two intense weeks training Muay Thai and Western boxing. Between sweat, rhythm, and sea breeze, they share reflections on discipline, friendship, and the beauty of movement - with a soundtrack that connects Thai sounds to the flow of daily practice.
songs: Burna Boy - Cheat on Me (feat. Dave)

published.match_reason: A military coup in South Asia set against an episode about martial arts, discipline, and Southeast Asian rhythms creates a subtle dialogue between power, control, and personal resilience.

published.fact_text: On 24 March 1939, about 40 members of a pro-Nazi group in Liechtenstein marched from the village of Nendeln toward Vaduz in the 1939 Liechtenstein putsch, hoping to topple the government and trigger annexation by Germany. Their mini–coup failed almost as soon as it began, becoming a strange footnote in the story of European fascism.

episode.title: Morning Drops #17 - Steel City
episode.description: Gaia takes us on a short trip through Sheffield's sounds — from electronic textures to raw guitars and sharp storytelling. Four tracks, one city, different edges of the same story.
songs: The Human League - The Sound Of The Crowd

published.match_reason: A tiny, fringe coup attempt in a small European state pairs with an episode that explores the sharp, gritty narratives of a single industrial city—both zoom in on small places with outsized stories.

published.fact_text: On 24 March 1387, an English fleet defeated a combined Franco-Castilian-Flemish force in the Battle of Margate off the Kent coast. The chaotic running sea battle sprawled over several days, more like a series of chases and skirmishes than a single decisive clash.

episode.title: Morning Drops #31 - Sailing With Germans
episode.description: From the deck of a sailing boat, hosted by Yasmin and her special guests. A week at sea with six Germans, each with their own stories, songs, and ways of moving through the waves. From shared meals to unexpected laughter, from quiet resilience to sun-soaked joy — a collection of voices and tracks that reflect what it means to journey together.
songs: Ben Howard - Keep Your Head Up

published.match_reason: A medieval naval battle off the English coast pairs neatly with an episode set on a sailboat, transforming the idea of life at sea from clashing fleets to shared journeys and stories on the water.

published.fact_text: On 24 March 2023, an EF4 tornado tore through Rolling Fork and Silver City, Mississippi, carving a path of destruction that flattened homes and infrastructure in minutes. Radar and eyewitness accounts showed a violent, wedge-shaped storm that turned familiar streets into unrecognizable debris fields.

episode.title: Morning Drops #05 - Electric Shock
episode.description: An energizing episode with electrifying sounds to wake you up and get you moving. High energy tracks that deliver a jolt of musical electricity to start your day.

published.match_reason: A powerful tornado is nature’s own electric shock to the landscape, making an episode full of high-voltage guitars and sudden energy a fitting sonic mirror to atmospheric extremes.

published.fact_text: On 24 March 2018, Turkish Armed Forces and the Syrian National Army announced full control of Syria’s Afrin District, concluding the Afrin offensive. The operation displaced tens of thousands of civilians and reshuffled local power in a war already layered with shifting alliances.

episode.title: Evening Flows #01 - Yasmin - Crescendo in the Medina
episode.description: The first Evening Flow episode comes from Yasmin. It starts deep in the medina, slow, mysterious, textured, and grows into a liberating dance across sub-Saharan landscapes. A journey through ambient soundscapes, Arabic melodies, Afro-Latin rhythms, and funky highlife grooves.
songs: David Visan & Carlos Campos - Crystal Heart
Evan Mars - Ya Zareef Al Tool
Dele Sosimi & Medlar - You No Fit Touch Am (Medlar Remix)

published.match_reason: A battle over a Syrian district connects to an episode that moves through Arabic melodies and cross-cultural rhythms, hinting at how places of conflict also hold deep cultural and musical histories.

published.fact_text: On 24 March 2015, Germanwings Flight 9525 was deliberately crashed into the French Alps by its co-pilot, killing all 150 people on board. The tragedy forced aviation authorities worldwide to rethink cockpit security and mental health protocols for pilots.

episode.title: Morning Drops #14 - Slow Planet
episode.description: A pause. A quiet return to slower rhythms, sparked by a live set under the summer sky. A moment to breathe and settle into the week.

published.match_reason: In the shadow of a devastating air disaster, a gently calming, reflective episode offers a sonic counterweight, inviting slow breathing and contemplation in a world where safety can feel fragile.

published.fact_text: On 24 March, the world also marks the International Day for the Right to the Truth Concerning Gross Human Rights Violations, designated by the UN in honor of Óscar Romero. It’s a reminder that uncovering what really happened—names, dates, and stories—is itself a form of justice.

episode.title: Morning Drops #27 - Nomophobia
episode.description: Yasmin reflects on what happened when she recently lost her phone and why it felt freeing. A dive into the psychology of our relationship with smartphones: from nomophobia (the fear of being without a phone), to digital burnout and cognitive overload, to the surprising benefits of boredom and the brain's default mode network. An invitation to rethink how we connect and disconnect.
